ENGNew: Falling in Love with Rain Clouds
changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Why I Like Rainy Days"

TextENGNew: The weather forecast predicted not a single drop of rain for the entire week.
It's not that I dislike sunny days.
But when day after day brings nothing but the same unchanging sunshine, anyone would begin to long for rain clouds.
If monotonous blue skies stretched on for a week, a month, a year -- anyone would begin to long for rain clouds.
When a weather specialist lines up reams of past data and pronounces their verdict after careful analysis, such a forecast is not easily overturned.@kI understood that much.
...Yet precisely because of that, I found myself gazing up at the cloudless sky, hoping that perhaps -- just this once -- the forecast might be wrong.
Was I being contrary?
changeReason: JPN "天邪鬼" specifically means contrarian/perverse, not mischievous. "Contrary" is significantly more accurate to the self-characterization.

TextENGNew: I waited and waited, feeling almost suffocated by the tedium of an interminably clear sky unmarred by a single cloud.
If one could actually suffocate from that...@kthen the human population would never have grown this large.
Which meant... the only person being suffocated by this was me.
And so...
...That is why I rejoice at a sudden summer shower -- one that not even the weather forecast had foreseen.
If I explained it this way, would you understand my feelings a little better?
Let's say that tonight's dinner was going to be curry rice.
But, when you're called down to the dinner table, what was waiting for you was instead an eggplant and bell pepper stirfry.
This is just on your mother's whim, by the way.
Such whimsy would delight me.@kI don't particularly care for eggplant or bell peppers, and yet I would be delighted all the same.
What made it wonderful was that the preordained harmony of curry rice for dinner had been broken.
changeReason: JPN "予定調和" (yotei chouwa) is a specific concept meaning "preordained harmony" (pre-established harmony from Leibniz), not simply a routine. The new translation preserves this philosophical nuance.

TextENGNew: If tonight was repeated a hundred times, and a hundred times you had to eat curry rice...
Yet for just this one night, that was changed to an eggplant and bell pepper stirfry,@k there's no way you couldn't enjoy that chance happening.
I hate preordained harmony.
changeReason: Same as above: 予定調和 means "preordained harmony," a philosophical concept distinct from mere "routines."

TextENGNew: I despise predetermined schedules.
I have no love for tedium.
I always get my hopes up that something today will be different from yesterday, no matter how trivial.
It's been decided that this entire week starting today was going to be clear skies.
The weather forecast decided upon it, so the God of Weather must also feel obliged to do so.
But who's to say that a rain cloud might not just show up one day on a whim?
...It is precisely because that element of uncertainty still remains in this world that a creature like me can go on breathing.
Tomorrow will probably be a hot and clear day.
But I alone know that this predetermined fate, with a probability of less than one percent... sometimes changes.
Hoping for that one percent, I hang upside-down teru-teru-bouzu from the sunny eaves.
In the end, I live in constant anticipation of just such surprises in all things.
I pondered why I was hoping for it...
Why was I hoping for a rain cloud?
...The answer was simple.@kI had my fill of clear skies.
Then why was I hoping for a rain cloud?
...The answer was simple.@kThe certainty that tomorrow would be sunny was unbearable.
changeReason: New translation renders 退屈 as "unbearable" which is significantly stronger than the JPN meaning of "boring/tedious," potentially overstating the speaker's feeling.

TextENGNew: So why was I hoping for a rain cloud?
In the end, it doesn't really matter whether it rains or shines tomorrow.
In the end, even that rain would merely dampen and soothe my heart, parched as it was by boredom.
And so, rather than watching a television drama with a predetermined plot...
...I prefer to gaze up at the sky.
